title: Protein Intake Calculator – Daily Grams
shortDescription: Estimate your daily protein needs from body weight and activity.
description: Enter your body weight and activity level to estimate daily protein in grams. Runs in your browser — no signup.
metaDescription: Free protein intake calculator. Enter your body weight and activity level to estimate how many grams of protein you need per day, from sedentary to athlete.

result.label: Daily protein target
result.empty: Enter your weight to estimate daily protein.
result.note: Based on {factor} g of protein per kg of body weight.

sections.heading: How much protein do you need?
paragraphs: Protein needs scale with body weight and activity. A common guide is 0.8 g per kg for sedentary adults (the RDA), 1.2–1.6 g/kg for active people, and up to 2.0 g/kg for athletes building or maintaining muscle. This calculator multiplies your weight by the factor for your activity level.

sections.heading: Using the result
paragraphs: The result is a daily target in grams. Spread it across meals — roughly 20–40 g per meal is well used by the body. Whole foods like eggs, dairy, meat, fish, legumes, and tofu are reliable sources. Athletes and older adults often benefit from the higher end of the range.

howTo.name: Enter your weight
howTo.text: Type your body weight in kilograms.

howTo.name: Choose activity level
howTo.text: Pick sedentary, lightly active, active, or athlete.

howTo.name: Read the target
howTo.text: See your estimated daily protein in grams.

faq.q: How much protein do I need per day?
faq.a: Roughly 0.8 g/kg if sedentary, 1.2–1.6 g/kg if active, and up to 2.0 g/kg for athletes.
Beyond your needs there is little extra benefit for most people, and very high intakes offer diminishing returns.

faq.q: Should I use body weight or lean mass?
faq.a: This tool uses total body weight, which is the most common guideline basis. Lean-mass methods exist but need a body-fat estimate.
Spreading intake across meals (about 20–40 g each) supports muscle better than one large serving.
No. It is a general estimate. Consult a doctor or dietitian for personalized targets, especially with kidney conditions.
